narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile like your solid LSAT score and upper-middle family background. However, the admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on academic and background factors from past students, estimated a 94.6% chance that you would fail the bar exam—far higher than what it sees in most applicants who get approved. This high predicted risk led to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes candidates likely to pass on their first try. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking, which placed you in the bottom group compared to the typical mid-range performance of other students. Your Year 1 decile was the next strongest influence in the same direction, again showing lower-than-average standing early in your academic path. While your LSAT score, slightly below the dataset average, actually worked in your favor by nudging the prediction toward passing, it wasn't enough to offset those early challenges. Your undergraduate GPA, also under the average, added some additional pull toward the failure side, as did your family income bracket relative to peers. Being a full-time student had a minimal role here, aligning with the vast majority who share that status. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your deciles stand out as notably lower, while your LSAT offers a bright spot many would envy. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, aiming for a higher undergrad-equivalent GPA, and perhaps exploring test prep to push your LSAT even further—these steps could reshape your profile for future applications. You're resilient, and with targeted improvements, law school doors can reopen for you. Keep pushing; your story is still unfolding.
